name: MoE
oneliner: Replace each FFN with N parallel expert FFNs, activate only top-k per token via learned routing. Decouples total params from per-token compute.
explanation: Each FFN layer becomes N expert FFNs + a router. Router selects top-k experts per token (typically k=1 or k=2). You get massive model capacity at fraction of compute cost. Key tradeoff: ALL params must be in memory even though only k/N are active. Mixtral 8x7B: 46.7B total, 12.9B active (8 experts, top-2). DeepSeek-V3: 671B total, 37B active (1 shared + 256 routed, top-8). Load balancing loss prevents expert collapse. Shared experts (DeepSeek) always fire as safety net alongside routed experts.
fundamentals: Router: g(x) = softmax(W_g·x), select TopK(g(x), k). Output: y = Σᵢ∈S g(x)ᵢ·Expertᵢ(x). Load balance loss: L = α·N·Σ(fᵢ·pᵢ) where fᵢ=token fraction, pᵢ=mean prob. Capacity factor CF: buffer = CF×(T/N) tokens per expert, excess dropped. Mixtral math: 32 layers × 8 experts × ~134M params/expert = 34.4B FFN + 12.3B attention = 46.7B total. Per token: 2/8 experts → 12.9B active. Memory: 46.7B (all loaded). FLOPS: ~13B equivalent.
sources: Shazeer et al., 'Outrageously Large Neural Networks,' ICLR 2017
Fedus et al., 'Switch Transformers,' JMLR 2022
Jiang et al., 'Mixtral of Experts,' arXiv:2401.04088
DeepSeek-AI, 'DeepSeek-V3,' arXiv:2412.19437

foundational_papers.title: Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer
Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion Parameter Models

name: Dense Models
expansion: Dense Model (all parameters active per token)
oneliner: 100% of parameters active for every token — the standard transformer design. Simpler than MoE but compute scales linearly with param count.
explanation: The default transformer architecture: every attention head and FFN layer processes every token. Term became necessary to distinguish from MoE/sparse. Advantages: simplicity (no routing instabilities, no load balancing, simpler serving), sample efficiency (every param sees every token). Disadvantage: compute = O(params). Preferred when: memory is the bottleneck and you want max quality per byte, simpler infrastructure needed, fine-tuning, or single-GPU deployment. Chinchilla scaling: N params should train on ~20N tokens.
fundamentals: Per-token FLOPS ≈ 2 × total_params (rough approximation). 70B dense model: ~140 TFLOPS/token. Same capacity as MoE ~280B total/70B active — same compute, 4× less memory. When model card lists single param count without total/active distinction → dense. Examples: LLaMA 3 8B/70B/405B, Mistral 7B, Qwen 2.5 72B, Gemma 31B.
sources: Vaswani et al., 'Attention Is All You Need,' 2017
Hoffmann et al., 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla),' arXiv:2203.15556

name: Knowledge Distillation
oneliner: Train a small 'student' to mimic a large 'teacher's output distributions — transfers 'dark knowledge' from soft probabilities that hard labels miss.
explanation: Hinton et al. (2015). Teacher's soft probability distribution over vocabulary contains far more information than one-hot labels — inter-class relationships, relative similarities. Student trained on combined loss: cross-entropy vs ground truth + KL divergence vs teacher's softened outputs (temperature T=2-20 amplifies dark knowledge). Modern LLM distillation often uses synthetic data generation instead of logit matching. Typical retention: 85-95% of teacher quality at 1/10th size. Examples: LLaMA 3.2 1B/3B from 8B/70B, Phi models from GPT-4-class, Gemma distilled variants.
fundamentals: L = α·CE(y_true, σ(z_s)) + (1-α)·T²·KL(σ(z_t/T) || σ(z_s/T)). Temperature T softens distributions. T² compensates gradient magnitude. Hard labels: ~15 bits/example (log₂(32K)). Soft labels: 32K continuous values — orders of magnitude more info. Distillation vs pruning: pruning removes params from existing model (architecture shrinks); distillation trains new smaller model from scratch using teacher signal. Can combine: prune then distill to recover (LLaMA 3.2 approach).
sources: Hinton et al., 'Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network,' 2015, arXiv:1503.02531
Meta, 'The Llama 3 Herd of Models,' arXiv:2407.21783

foundational_papers.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network

name: Speculative Decoding
oneliner: Small draft model proposes K candidate tokens, large model verifies all K in one forward pass. 2-3× speedup with provably identical output distribution.
explanation: Exploits the asymmetry: LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound, so processing 1 token or 5 tokens costs nearly the same. Draft model (10-50× fewer params) generates K candidates autoregressively. Target model verifies all K in single forward pass via modified rejection sampling. If draft token matches target distribution: accept. If not: reject and sample correction from adjusted distribution. Output is mathematically identical to standard sampling — not approximate. Acceptance rates 70-85% → 2-3× speedup. Supported by vLLM, TGI, llama.cpp, TensorRT-LLM.
fundamentals: Verification per position i: accept draft token x with prob min(1, p(x)/q(x)) where p=target, q=draft. If rejected: sample from p'(x) = max(0, p(x)-q(x)) / Z. Expected tokens per step: (1-α^(K+1))/(1-α) where α=acceptance rate. For K=5, α=0.8: ~3.36 tokens/step → ~2.7× speedup. Draft model can be: smaller same-family model, separate small model, or even n-gram model. Self-speculative: use target model with early exit for drafting.
sources: Leviathan et al., 'Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding,' ICML 2023, arXiv:2211.17192
Chen et al., 'Accelerating LLM Decoding with Speculative Sampling,' arXiv:2302.01318

foundational_papers.title: Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding
Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling

name: Expert Routing / Gating
oneliner: The learned mechanism assigning tokens to experts — linear projection → softmax → top-k selection. Load balancing is the central challenge.
explanation: Router must: (a) send tokens to relevant experts, (b) balance load across experts, (c) be differentiable for end-to-end training. Standard: linear gate W_g·x → softmax → top-k. Without regularization, router collapses to few popular experts. Fixes: auxiliary load-balancing loss (penalizes uneven utilization), noisy gating (Gaussian noise for exploration), expert choice routing (experts choose tokens, guarantees balance). DeepSeek innovation: shared experts (always fire) + routed experts (selected) + bias-based load balancing (avoids aux loss entirely).
fundamentals: Top-k: scores = W_g·x, probs = softmax(scores), select top-k, output = Σ wᵢ·Expertᵢ(x). Noisy gating: scores += softplus(W_noise·x)·ε. Load balance loss: L = α·N·Σ(fᵢ·pᵢ). Expert choice (Zhou 2022): each expert picks top-c tokens from batch — perfect balance by construction. DeepSeek shared+routed: y = Expert_shared(x) + Σᵢ∈TopK gᵢ·Expert_routed_i(x). DeepSeek-V3 bias-based balance: routing_score_i = (W_g·x)ᵢ + bᵢ, biases updated from running load stats.
sources: Shazeer et al., 'Outrageously Large Neural Networks,' ICLR 2017
Zhou et al., 'Mixture-of-Experts with Expert Choice Routing,' NeurIPS 2022
DeepSeek-AI, 'DeepSeek-V2,' arXiv:2405.04434

name: Continuous Batching
oneliner: Dynamically insert/remove requests from a running batch at each generation step — eliminates wasted slots from variable-length outputs. 2-4× throughput gain.
explanation: Static batching: collect N requests, process until ALL finish, then accept new batch. Short requests waste their slots while waiting for long ones. Continuous batching (Orca, Yu et al. 2022): after each token step, remove finished requests and fill slots with waiting ones. Synergistic with PagedAttention: dynamic KV cache allocation/freeing enables efficient slot reuse. Together: 2-4× throughput vs static batching, up to 23× under high load (vLLM paper). Default in vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM.
fundamentals: Per-iteration scheduler: 1) Generate one token for all active requests. 2) Remove finished (EOS/max_len), free KV cache. 3) Fill freed slots with new requests (run prefill). 4) Repeat. Prefill vs decode asymmetry: prefill is compute-bound (many tokens), decode is memory-bound (one token). Advanced: chunked prefill interleaves prefill chunks with decode steps. Concrete: static batching wastes 60-80% KV memory on fragmentation; PagedAttention reduces to <4%.
sources: Yu et al., 'Orca: A Distributed Serving System,' OSDI 2022
Kwon et al., 'PagedAttention,' SOSP 2023, arXiv:2309.06180

foundational_papers.title: Orca: A Distributed Serving System for Transformer-Based Generative Models

name: Tensor Parallelism (TP) vs Pipeline Parallelism (PP)
expansion: TP = Tensor (Intra-Layer) Parallelism; PP = Pipeline (Inter-Layer) Parallelism
oneliner: TP splits each layer across GPUs (every GPU computes a slice of every layer). PP assigns different layers to different GPUs (sequential pipeline). TP within nodes, PP across nodes.
explanation: TP (Megatron-LM, 2019): split weight matrices across GPUs. Column-parallel up-projection, row-parallel down-projection. Requires 2 AllReduce ops per layer — needs NVLink-class bandwidth (~900 GB/s). Best within a single node (TP=8 on DGX H100). PP: layers 0-15 on GPU 0, layers 16-31 on GPU 1. Point-to-point communication (lower bandwidth OK). Suffers pipeline bubbles: bubble fraction = (P-1)/(M+P-1) with M micro-batches. Best across nodes. Large-scale uses 3D parallelism: TP within node + PP across nodes + DP across replicas. Expert Parallelism (EP) is a third dimension for MoE.
fundamentals: TP: W split column-wise into [W₁|...|Wₚ]. GPU_i computes Y_i = X·W_i. AllGather to reconstruct or AllReduce to sum. 2 AllReduces per layer, each ~2M bytes for hidden_dim M. PP: micro-batch pipeline. Bubble = (P-1)/(M+P-1). For P=8, M=32: 18% bubble. Interleaved schedules: non-contiguous layer assignment reduces bubbles by factor v. Inference: prefer TP (minimizes latency — all GPUs work on every token). Training: 3D parallelism.
sources: Narayanan et al., 'Megatron-LM,' arXiv:1909.08053, arXiv:2104.04473
Huang et al., 'GPipe,' NeurIPS 2019, arXiv:1811.06965

foundational_papers.title: Efficient Large-Scale Language Model Training on GPU Clusters Using Megatron-LM
GPipe: Efficient Training of Giant Neural Networks using Pipeline Parallelism

name: Model Merging
expansion: Model Merging / Weight Merging / Model Fusion
oneliner: Combine weights from multiple fine-tuned models (same base) by arithmetic interpolation — no training needed. Popular methods: SLERP, TIES, DARE.
explanation: Fine-tuned models from the same base occupy nearby loss landscape regions → interpolating weights lands on a point combining both capabilities. Simplest: linear averaging (Model Soups). SLERP: spherical interpolation respecting high-dim geometry. TIES: trim small changes, resolve sign conflicts via majority vote, then merge. DARE: randomly drop 90% of changes, rescale survivors, then merge — exploits redundancy in task vectors. Works for complementary specializations (code + creative writing). Fails when: no shared base, conflicting capabilities, too much fine-tuning. Tool: mergekit (arcee-ai). Many Open LLM Leaderboard top models are merges.
fundamentals: Task vector: τ_A = W_A - W_base. Linear: W = W_base + α·τ_A + β·τ_B. SLERP: interpolate along great circle on unit hypersphere, per-tensor. TIES: 1) Trim entries below magnitude percentile (e.g., 80th). 2) Elect sign by majority vote across models. 3) Average agreed entries. DARE: 1) Drop entries with probability p (e.g., 0.9). 2) Rescale survivors by 1/(1-p). 3) Merge. Why it works: 'linear mode connectivity' — same-base fine-tunes stay in same loss basin. Breaks with high LR, many epochs, or very different tasks.
sources: Wortsman et al., 'Model Soups,' ICML 2022
Yadav et al., 'TIES-Merging,' NeurIPS 2023, arXiv:2306.01708
Yu et al., 'DARE,' ICML 2024, arXiv:2311.03099

foundational_papers.title: Model Soups: Averaging Weights of Multiple Fine-tuned Models
TIES-Merging: Resolving Interference When Merging Models
Language Models are Super Mario: Absorbing Abilities from Homologous Models as a Free Lunch (DARE)

name: Inference Engine
expansion: Specialized runtime for executing trained LLM weights
oneliner: Purpose-built software for running LLM forward passes at maximum efficiency — 5-20x faster than naive PyTorch via KV cache management, continuous batching, kernel fusion, and quantization support.
explanation: While you can serve an LLM with vanilla PyTorch model.generate(), inference engines exist because serving (many concurrent users, variable-length sequences, autoregressive decoding) is fundamentally different from training. Engines optimize the narrow path of repeated forward passes: KV cache management avoids recomputing past context, continuous batching dynamically groups requests so GPUs never idle, kernel fusion merges operations to reduce memory round-trips, quantization support runs INT4/INT8/FP8 with purpose-built kernels. Key engines: vLLM (PagedAttention, most adopted), TGI (HuggingFace), llama.cpp (CPU/consumer, GGUF format), TensorRT-LLM (NVIDIA, highest throughput), ExLlamaV2 (extreme quantization), SGLang (structured generation), Ollama (user-friendly local), LM Studio (desktop GUI).
fundamentals: Core bottleneck: memory bandwidth, not compute. Each decode step reads ALL weights from GPU memory for ONE token. 70B in fp16 = 140 GB streaming through memory bus per token. A100 at 2 TB/s = ~70ms minimum. Engines attack this: quantization shrinks bytes read, KV caching avoids re-reading past context, batching amortizes the weight-read across many sequences. Two phases: prefill (processing prompt, compute-bound, parallel) vs decode (generating tokens, memory-bound, sequential). Good engines treat them differently — chunked prefill avoids blocking decode for other requests. Throughput vs latency tradeoff: larger batches improve throughput but increase per-request latency. Speculative decoding improves latency without sacrificing throughput.

foundational_papers.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention
Orca: A Distributed Serving System for Transformer-Based Generative Models
FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness

sources: Kwon et al., arXiv:2309.06180
Yu et al., OSDI 2022
